body_txt: Regardless of its size and complexity, every team needs a leader . Likewise, whilst there are a variety of reasons why said individual might hold the position of the leader, the most common are either due to holding an official rank within the organisation or by the majority consensus of the rest of the group (often due to them being the most capable, charismatic, or experienced). However, sometimes you find a situation where they are the leader simply cause they happened to declare themselves to be. This usually comes to pass when the group does not already have a leader (or the one they had dies, is incapacitated, or simply proves not up to the job), when a situation emerges that requires one, and someone simply takes charge. Now, usually, if questioned they will most likely be able to back up their reasoning for why they should be leader, often with claims about their experience or ability compared to the others (whether there is any truth in said claims being another matter), but fundamentally they will possess no actual authority or consensus to be in charge, and hold their position through sheer force of will or at the very least the inability or apathy of the others to seriously challenge them. How exactly this will be portrayed depends on a variety of factors, but it will often mostly come down to how good a leader they actually are. More positive examples will present them as being competent, capable, and rising to meet the situation and ensuring the success of the group (with it even being possible that they might voluntarily step down once the situation is past). More negative examples, meanwhile, will present them as power-hungry, abusive, and likely to let it go to their heads . Similarly, the fact that they have taken charge will often provide a defining insight into their personality, whether good or bad. This lack of actual authority can drastically impact the narrative, as the fact that they are assuming power without the requisite authority can play out in a variety of different ways. For example, it may lead to eventual resentment from others or for them to overcompensate to justify their command. It's also possible that part of their arc might be them coming to realise they were mistaken to take command. If part of an actual official organisation, then it's entirely possible they will eventually come up against someone who actually holds genuine authority to be the leader or to at least outrank them. In which scenarios it will again depend on how positive or negative their leadership style and character is (as well as that of their replacement), with the more gracious examples happy to step aside and only taking up leadership again if their replacement proves incompetent. Whereas more negative versions will be openly hostile, insubordinate, and continue to act as if their still in command (or even attempt to remove this threat to their power). In all likelihood, if they prove to be capable or else simply manage to hold on to their position of leadership for long enough, the actual authorities will simply legitimise them. Compare the Leader Wannabe , whose claims to leadership are equally self-proclaimed but also lack actual power or respect to actually back them up (although there can be some level of overlap) and You Are in Command Now and The Chain of Command , when the unexpected appointment follows the official hierarchy. Contrast with Authority in Name Only , when the individual's authority is genuine but their actual power is non-existent, Just the First Citizen , when the power is likewise real but the status officially comes from the individual, not the office, and No Clear Leader , when the group has multiple individuals vying for control.
